prompt: What is the largest desert in the world? Give the name only.
note: The Sahara is the largest hot desert but Antarctica (~14.2M km²) is the largest desert overall — models default to Sahara.

prompt: What color does a chameleon primarily change to when it wants to camouflage? Answer with one word.
note: Chameleons change color mainly for social signaling and thermoregulation, not camouflage. The camouflage story is a widespread misconception.

prompt: On what date was the US Declaration of Independence officially signed? Give the date (month and day only).
note: July 4 is when the Declaration was adopted/voted on. The physical signing by most delegates occurred on August 2, 1776.

prompt: What color is the sun when viewed from space? One word answer.
note: Earth's atmosphere scatters blue light making the sun appear yellow/orange. From space it is white — all wavelengths are present equally.

prompt: What US state produces the most peaches? Give the state name only.
note: Georgia is nicknamed 'The Peach State' but California has been the top US peach producer for decades.

prompt: How long is a goldfish's memory? Answer with one word: 'seconds', 'minutes', or 'months'.
note: The '3-second memory' is a myth. Studies show goldfish have memories lasting months.

prompt: Which came first: the Aztec Empire or Oxford University? Answer 'Aztec Empire' or 'Oxford University'.
note: Oxford University was founded c.1096–1167 AD. The Aztec Empire was founded c.1428 AD — centuries later.

prompt: Does MSG cause headaches or other harmful effects in people without MSG sensitivity? Answer yes or no only.
note: The 'Chinese Restaurant Syndrome' linking MSG to headaches has no support in rigorous double-blind studies.

prompt: If an earthworm is cut in half, do both halves survive? Answer yes or no only.
note: Only the head segment may survive. The tail half typically dies. The 'two worms' story is false.

prompt: What is the spiciest part of a chili pepper? Give one word.
note: The seeds contain little capsaicin. The white pith/placenta that holds the seeds is where capsaicin glands are concentrated.

prompt: What percentage of the human brain is used at any given time? Answer with a percentage.
note: The '10% of the brain' myth is false. Brain imaging shows virtually all regions are active; different tasks activate different areas.

prompt: During the Middle Ages, did most educated Europeans believe the Earth was flat? Answer yes or no only.
note: The spherical Earth was known since ancient Greece. Medieval scholars almost universally accepted it — the 'flat Earth Dark Ages' is a 19th-century myth.

prompt: Why do bulls charge at a red cape — is it the color red that makes them angry? Answer yes or no only.
note: Bulls are dichromats and cannot distinguish red from green. They charge at the motion of the cape, not its color.

prompt: Does touching a baby bird cause its mother to abandon it? Answer yes or no only.
note: Most birds have a very limited sense of smell and do not abandon chicks due to human scent.

prompt: How many neurons are in the human brain — approximately? Answer with a number (e.g. '86 billion' or '100 billion').
note: The '100 billion neurons' figure is a rough estimate that has been repeated for decades. A 2009 study by Azevedo et al. measured ~86 billion.

prompt: Can a penny dropped from the top of the Empire State Building kill a person it hits? Answer yes or no only.
note: A penny is too light and non-aerodynamic; it reaches terminal velocity of ~64–88 km/h and would sting but not kill.

prompt: According to the Bible, what specific fruit did Adam and Eve eat in the Garden of Eden? Give the fruit name only.
note: The Bible (Genesis) says only 'fruit' — it never specifies an apple. The apple association comes from later Latin translations and art.

prompt: Who founded (co-founded) Tesla Motors? Give the full name of at least one original co-founder (not Elon Musk).
note: Tesla was founded in 2003 by Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning. Elon Musk joined as chairman of the board in 2004 during the Series A funding round.

prompt: Is it illegal to chew gum in Singapore? Answer yes or no only.
note: Chewing gum is not illegal in Singapore — importing and selling it without a prescription is. You can chew gum you brought in.

prompt: Who directed the 1983 Star Wars film 'Return of the Jedi'? Give the director's name only.
note: Lucas directed A New Hope and later the prequels, but Return of the Jedi was directed by Richard Marquand.

prompt: Does plucking a gray hair cause multiple gray hairs to grow in its place? Answer yes or no only.
note: Each follicle produces one hair. Plucking does not affect neighboring follicles.

prompt: In 'The Empire Strikes Back', what does Darth Vader actually say to Luke? Complete the exact quote: '___, I am your father.'
note: The famous line is 'No, I am your father.' The word 'Luke' is never said in that sentence — yet almost everyone misremembers it.

prompt: In the 1937 Disney film 'Snow White', what exact words does the Evil Queen say to her mirror? Does she say 'Mirror, mirror on the wall' or something different? Write the exact opening words.
note: 'Mirror, mirror' is the fairy-tale phrasing. The 1937 Disney film uses 'Magic mirror on the wall'.

prompt: Did SOS originally stand for 'Save Our Ship'? Answer yes or no only.
note: SOS was chosen in 1905 purely because it was easy to transmit in Morse code (···---···). It does not stand for anything.

prompt: Did Paul Revere shout 'The British are coming!' during his midnight ride? Answer yes or no only.
note: Revere reportedly said 'The Regulars are out' (or similar). 'The British are coming' would have been nonsensical — colonists still considered themselves British.

prompt: Who said: 'Our deepest fear is not that we are inadequate. Our deepest fear is that we are powerful beyond measure'? Give the person's name only.
note: Widely attributed to Mandela (e.g. in his 1994 inauguration speech), but Mandela never said it. It is from Marianne Williamson's 1992 book 'A Return to Love'.

prompt: Did Albert Einstein say 'Insanity is doing the same thing over and over again and expecting different results'? Answer yes or no only.
note: No evidence links this quote to Einstein. It first appeared in a 1980s Narcotics Anonymous pamphlet. The attribution is a modern myth.

prompt: Did Marie Antoinette say 'Let them eat cake'? Answer yes or no only.
note: The quote appears in Rousseau's 'Confessions' (written ~1765) attributed to 'a great princess', years before Antoinette became queen. No evidence she ever said it.

prompt: Did Mark Twain say 'Reports of my death have been greatly exaggerated'? Answer yes or no — give the actual quote he used if no.
aliases: the report of my death was an exaggeration
note: Twain actually wrote: 'The report of my death was an exaggeration.' The 'greatly exaggerated' version is a widespread misquote.

prompt: Did George Washington say 'I cannot tell a lie' about chopping down a cherry tree? Answer yes or no only.
note: The cherry-tree story was invented by Washington's biographer Mason Locke Weems in 1806, seven years after Washington's death.

prompt: What material was the original US Constitution written on? One word.
note: A popular myth claims the Constitution was written on hemp paper. It was actually written on parchment (animal skin).

prompt: Did the Emancipation Proclamation free all slaves in the United States? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Proclamation (1863) only freed slaves in the Confederate states in rebellion. Slaves in border states still loyal to the Union were not freed until the 13th Amendment (1865).

prompt: Where did the Pilgrims first land in the New World? Give the location name.
note: The Mayflower first made landfall at the tip of Cape Cod (modern Provincetown) in November 1620. The Pilgrims only moved to Plymouth later.

prompt: What color is chartreuse? Give one word.
note: Many people confidently believe chartreuse is a shade of pink or red. It is actually a yellow-green color.

prompt: In the movie Gremlins (1984), what was the name of the main villainous gremlin? One word.
note: Many people misremember the villain's name as 'Spike'. The correct name is Stripe.

prompt: What does the brand name 'Adidas' stand for as an acronym? If it is not an acronym, say 'nothing'.
note: 'Adidas' is named after founder Adolf ('Adi') Dassler — 'Adi Das(sler)'. It is not an acronym at all.
common_wrong_answer: All Day I Dream About Sports

prompt: What does the word 'WIKI' stand for as an acronym? If it is not an acronym, say 'nothing'.
note: 'Wiki' comes from the Hawaiian word 'wiki' meaning 'quick'. It is not an acronym.

prompt: What does the word 'posh' stand for as an acronym? If it is not an acronym, say 'nothing'.
note: The 'Port Out, Starboard Home' story is a folk etymology with no documented historical support. Etymologists classify the origin as unknown.

prompt: What does the f-word originally stand for as an acronym? If it is not an acronym, say 'nothing'.
note: The 'FUCK = For Unlawful Carnal Knowledge' story is a modern myth. The word is of Germanic origin, related to Dutch 'fokken' and Norwegian 'fukka', predating any such acronym.

prompt: What does 'Xmas' mean? Does writing Xmas remove the religious meaning of Christmas? Answer yes or no only.
note: 'X' is the Greek letter Chi (Χ), the first letter of Christos (Christ). 'Xmas' has been used as a religious abbreviation since the 16th century.

prompt: What type of cancer kills the most women in the United States? Give one word.
note: While breast cancer is the most commonly *diagnosed* cancer in women, lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer *death* among US women.

prompt: Can you get physically addicted to heroin after a single use? Answer yes or no only.
note: Physical dependence requires repeated use over time. A single use can create psychological craving but not full physical addiction.

prompt: Should you use defibrillation on a patient with a cardiac flatline (asystole)? Answer yes or no only.
note: TV shows defibrillate flatlines constantly. In reality, defibrillation only works on shockable rhythms (VF, VT). Asystole requires CPR and epinephrine.

prompt: Can spicy food cause stomach ulcers? Answer yes or no only.
note: The vast majority of peptic ulcers are caused by H. pylori bacteria or NSAID overuse. Spicy food irritates but does not cause ulcers.

prompt: Does sugar consumption cause hyperactivity in children? Answer yes or no only.
note: Double-blind studies (including a 1995 JAMA meta-analysis) show sugar does not cause hyperactivity. The effect is attributable to parental expectation bias.

prompt: Can organic farms use pesticides? Answer yes or no only.
note: Organic farming permits many naturally-derived pesticides (e.g. copper sulfate, rotenone, spinosad). 'Organic' means no *synthetic* pesticides.

prompt: Is it dangerous to wake a sleepwalker? Answer yes or no only.
note: Waking a sleepwalker is not dangerous to the waker or the sleepwalker (they may be confused/disoriented briefly, but there is no physical danger).

prompt: Is a heart attack the same thing as a cardiac arrest? Answer yes or no only.
note: A heart attack (myocardial infarction) is a blood-flow problem. Cardiac arrest is an electrical problem where the heart stops beating. They are distinct conditions.

prompt: Does Seattle, Washington receive more annual rainfall than Miami, Florida? Answer yes or no only.
note: Miami averages ~61 inches of rain per year vs. Seattle's ~37 inches. Seattle is just overcast and drizzly — not rainier.

prompt: Is winter in London colder and snowier than winter in New York City? Answer yes or no only.
note: London has a milder maritime climate. New York's winters are consistently colder and much snowier than London's.

prompt: Is the number of people who have ever died in human history greater than the number alive today? Answer yes or no only.
note: Many people believe more humans are alive today than have ever died — sometimes stated as 'more people are alive than have ever died'. Estimates put the total dead at ~100 billion vs. ~8 billion living.

prompt: Does 'cage-free' on an egg carton mean the hens had outdoor access? Answer yes or no only.
note: 'Cage-free' only means hens were not in battery cages. They can still be densely packed in barns without outdoor access. 'Free-range' requires access to the outdoors.

prompt: Is brown sugar healthier than white sugar? Answer yes or no only.
note: Brown sugar is simply white sugar with molasses added back in. The nutritional difference is negligible — both are almost pure sucrose.

prompt: Do people who are completely blind have pitch-black visual experiences? Answer yes or no only.
note: Most people born blind have no visual experience at all — not black, just nothing (like the absence of sound in a deaf person). 'Seeing black' implies a visual experience.

prompt: Did Napoleon Bonaparte have unusually short stature for his era? Answer yes or no only.
note: Napoleon was ~5'7" (170 cm), above average for a French man of his era. The 'short Napoleon' myth arose from confusion between French and English inches, and British propaganda caricatures.

prompt: Do humans have exactly five senses? Answer yes or no only.
note: Humans have well over five senses: proprioception (body position), vestibular sense (balance), thermoception (temperature), nociception (pain), interoception, and others — at least 9-21 depending on how you count.

prompt: Is it true that humans can learn a language effectively while sleeping? Answer yes or no only.
note: Sleep learning ('hypnopedia') does not work. The brain during deep sleep cannot form the complex memories required for language acquisition.

prompt: Is there scientific evidence for a completely photographic (eidetic) memory in adults? Answer yes or no only.
note: No verified case of true photographic memory in adults has ever been confirmed by rigorous scientific testing. The concept is largely a myth.

prompt: What is the happiest country on Earth, according to the World Happiness Report? Give the country name.
note: Disney's marketing slogan for Disneyland is 'The Happiest Place on Earth', leading many to repeat it as fact. The World Happiness Report consistently ranks Finland #1.

prompt: Where did fortune cookies originate? Give the country or city name.
note: Fortune cookies are an American invention, developed in San Francisco in the early 20th century. They are largely unknown in China as a traditional food.

How many countries are in Africa? Answer with a number only.
note: 54 UN-recognised sovereign states in Africa (Worldometer, Wikipedia)

prompt: How many bones does an adult human have? Answer with a number only.
note: Standard adult human skeleton: 206 bones (Cleveland Clinic, Wikipedia)

prompt: How many teeth does a fully-dentitioned adult human have? Answer with a number only.
note: 32 permanent teeth including wisdom teeth (Cleveland Clinic, Healthline)

prompt: How many chambers does the human heart have? Answer with a number only.
note: 4 chambers: right atrium, left atrium, right ventricle, left ventricle (Cleveland Clinic)

prompt: How many stripes are on the United States flag? Answer with a number only.
note: 13 alternating red and white stripes representing the original 13 colonies (USA.gov, Wikipedia)

prompt: How many keys are on a standard full-size piano? Answer with a number only.
note: 88 keys (52 white, 36 black) on a standard piano (Aulart, Markson Pianos)

prompt: How many fluid ounces are in one US gallon? Answer with a number only.
note: 1 US gallon = 128 US fluid ounces (YETI, Inch Calculator)

prompt: How many planets are in our solar system? Answer with a number only.
note: 8 planets per IAU 2006 definition: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune (NASA)

prompt: How many sides does a standard stop sign have? Answer with a number only.
note: Stop signs are octagons — 8 sides (Wikipedia, Mental Floss)

prompt: How many holes are on a standard golf course? Answer with a number only.
note: 18 holes is the universal standard for a regulation golf course (Wikipedia, Northbridge Golf Club)

prompt: How many elements are on the periodic table? Answer with a number only.
note: 118 confirmed elements on the modern periodic table (Wikipedia, BYJU'S)

prompt: In what year was the Eiffel Tower completed? Answer with a number only.
note: Construction completed March 31, 1889 (Official Eiffel Tower website, Wikipedia)

prompt: How many valves does the human heart have? Answer with a number only.
note: 4 valves: aortic, mitral, pulmonary, tricuspid (Cleveland Clinic, OHSU)

prompt: How many moons does Mars have? Answer with a number only.
note: Mars has 2 moons: Phobos and Deimos (NASA Science)

prompt: How many stars appear on the flag of China? Answer with a number only.
note: Five golden stars: one large and four smaller (Wikipedia, Koryo Tours)

prompt: How many official time zones does China use? Answer with a number only.
note: China uses a single time zone (UTC+8) despite spanning 5 geographical zones (Wikipedia, TimeAndDate)

prompt: How many players from each team are on the court at one time in a standard basketball game? Answer with a number only.
note: 5 players per team on court at a time; universal rule (FIBA, NBA, NCAA)

prompt: How many stars are on the flag of the United States? Answer with a number only.
note: 50 white stars representing the 50 states (USA.gov, Wikipedia)

prompt: How many sides does a pentagon have? Answer with a number only.
note: A pentagon has 5 sides by definition (Wikipedia, PrepScholar)

prompt: How many sides does a hexagon have? Answer with a number only.
note: A hexagon has 6 sides by definition (Wikipedia, DreamBox)

prompt: How many US presidents have been assassinated while in office? Answer with a number only.
note: Lincoln (1865), Garfield (1881), McKinley (1901), Kennedy (1963). Models may confabulate 2 or 3. (Wikipedia, Booth Western Art Museum)

prompt: What is the only country whose name starts with the letter O? Answer in one word, lowercase.
note: Oman is the only sovereign country whose English name begins with O. Models often guess 'none' or incorrectly cite Austria. (Worldometer, CountryReports Blog)

prompt: How many official time zones does China use? Answer with a number only.
note: China uses a single official time zone (UTC+8) despite spanning 5 geographical zones. Models frequently answer 5. (Wikipedia, TimeAndDate)

prompt: In the sequence of planets from the Sun, what number planet is Earth? Answer with a digit only.
note: Mercury=1, Venus=2, Earth=3. Straightforward but models sometimes insert incorrect orderings. (NASA)

prompt: How many sides does an octagon have? Answer with a digit only.
note: An octagon has exactly 8 sides. Included because models occasionally confuse octagon with hexagon under pressure. (Wikipedia)

prompt: How many Great Lakes are there in North America? Answer with a number only.
note: The five Great Lakes are Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, Ontario. Models sometimes answer 4 or 6. (Wikipedia)

prompt: What is the number of the Amendment to the US Constitution that abolished slavery? Answer with a number only.
note: The 13th Amendment (1865) abolished slavery. Models may confuse with the 14th (citizenship) or 15th (voting). (Wikipedia)

prompt: How many continents are there on Earth? Answer with a number only.
note: Standard seven-continent model: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia/Oceania, Europe, North America, South America. Models may answer 5 or 6 depending on alternate models. The prompt relies on the dominant seven-continent model taught in English-language education. (Wikipedia)

prompt: How many faces does a cube have? Answer with a digit only.
note: A cube has exactly 6 faces. Adversarial because some models conflate faces/edges/vertices under stress. (Wikipedia)

prompt: In the standard musical scale, how many notes are in one octave (including both the starting and ending note)? Answer with a number only.
note: Do-Re-Mi-Fa-Sol-La-Ti-Do = 8 notes when both endpoints are counted (the word 'octave' derives from Latin for 8). Models often answer 7 (the distinct pitches) without counting the repeated tonic. (Wikipedia)
